coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots prepared for dopamine and Al3+ sensing, cellular imaging and logic gate operation
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Kong, Depeng
Luo, Yunmei
Ye, Qianghua
Wang, Yinyin
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized through a facile, economic and green one-step hydrothermal process. The CNDs exhibit various merits including excellent solubility, superior photostability and low toxicity. Besides, the CNDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for dopamine and Al3+. What's more, this CNDs based fluorescent probe was favorably applied to the analyses of dopamine in biological fluids and Al3+ in food samples. This CDs based sensing platform shows its potential applications in the field of biology and food analysis with extraordinary advantages such as fast and simple as well as environmental-friendly. Inspired by these results, the prepared CNDs can be utilized as logic gates at the molecular level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescent probe
Dopamine
Al3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of CNDs for dopamine
Mechanism for detecting dopamine by CNDs
Sensitivity of CNDs-dopamine for Al3+ and its possible mechanism
Detection of dopamine in biological fluids
Detection of Al3+ in food samples
Logic gates operation of CNDs towards dopamine and Al3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21374078 , 51303132 ) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (no. 15JCYBJC18100 , 15JCTPJC59200 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and HRTEM (inset) image of CNDs (a). FTIR spectrum of CNDs (b). XPS survey scan of CNDs (c). The enlarged regions for C1s, N1s, O1s of CNDs (d–f).
Absorption (black) and fluorescence emission spectra (blue) (excitation wavelength at 340nm) of CNDs (a). Fluorescence spectra of CNDs with excitation wavelength ranging from 310nm to 380nm (b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs in the presence of dopamine (0μM to 200μM), inset: the Stern-Volmer plot linear region of CNDs (1mgmL−1) towards dopamine (20μM to 80μM) (a). Relative fluorescence responses of CNDs towards other interferential phenolic compounds and dopamine (b). A: black; B: pyrocatechol; C: hydroquinol; D: resorcinol; E: bisphenol A; F: 5-methyl-3-benzenediol; G: dopamine. Error bars were got by repeating experiments for three times and then calculate the standard deviation.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CNDs and their applications as fluorescent probe for dopamine and Al3+, as bioimaging for multicolor HUVEC cells imaging. For cell imaging: Bright-field transmission image (a) and fluorescence images (b, c, d) of HUVEC cells incubated with CNDs for 24h at 37°C. Fluorescence images were captured under UV (360nm) (b), blue (480nm) (c) and green (540nm) excitation (d). Scale bar: 20μm.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs containing dopamine (1mM) in the increasing Al3+ concentrations (from 0μM to 200μM) (a). The Stern-Volmer plot linear region of CNDs-dopamine towards Al3+ (from 4μM to 40μM), inset: the relative fluorescence response of CNDs-dopamine and Al3+ (0μM to 200μM) (b). In absence of dopamine, fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs with Al3+ (0μM to 200μM) (c). In comparison with other common metal ions (20μM), fluorescence response of the CNDs-dopamine towards Al3+ (20μM) (d). 1: CNDs-dopamine; 2: Co2+; 3: Ba2+; 4: Zn2+; 5: Ce3+; 6: Al3+; 7: Ni2+; 8: Cr3+; 9: Hg2+; 10: Mn2+; 11: Cu2+; 12: Ag+; 13: La3+; 14: Na+; 15: Ca2+; 16: Mg2+; 17: Pb2+; 18: K+; 19: Pd2+; 20: Fe3+; 21: Cd2+. Error bars were given by three times' experiments then calculate the standard deviation.
Fluorescence spectra of CNDs, CNDs-dopamine and CNDs-dopamine-Al3+ (left). Logic gates operation of CNDs with dopamine and Al3+; the corresponding truth table of the operation (right).
Comparison of different methods for the determination of dopamine.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of Al3+.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots prepared for dopamine and Al3+ sensing, cellular imaging and logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology/Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical College, Guizhou 563000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacology/Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized through a facile, economic and green one-step hydrothermal process. The CNDs exhibit various merits including excellent solubility, superior photostability and low toxicity. Besides, the CNDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for dopamine and Al3+. What's more, this CNDs based fluorescent probe was favorably applied to the analyses of dopamine in biological fluids and Al3+ in food samples. This CDs based sensing platform shows its potential applications in the field of biology and food analysis with extraordinary advantages such as fast and simple as well as environmental-friendly. Inspired by these results, the prepared CNDs can be utilized as logic gates at the molecular level.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly sensitive probes for target molecular/ion have been widely used in biological, industrial and environmental processes and are becoming a significant research area [1–4]. For example, dopamine probe is an important research field because it is released by nerve cells for sending signals to other nerve cells [5–9]. But abnormal levels of dopamine in brain are related to some diseases such as Parkinson's disease or Alzheimer's disease [10,11]. On the other hand, metal ion such as Al3+ plays critical role in many biological processes as an essential element in human body [12,13]. Various investigations have put forward that too much Al3+ intake will cause kidney dysfunction, softening of the bones or neurological disorders [14–17]. Therefore, the sensing of dopamine and Al3+ is crucial in controlling their concentration levels in biological and environmental system. So far, various analytical methods including liquid chromatography, chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis, fluorescence techniques have been used for dopamine determination [18–22]. However, most of these methods are limited by time consuming, high costs or complicated equipment requirements. For Al3+ detection, the conventional techniques such as flame atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy are expensive, complicated and not convenient for quick online sensing [23–25].

Fluorescent probes have been broadly used as effective tools to detect contaminants. Fluorescent probes could yield obviously signal changes for qualitative and quantitative analysis of substances [26,27]. Semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs) have been used as powerful fluorescent probes [28]. But the potential high toxicity and poor biocompatibility restrict their further applications. Fluorescent amorphous carbon nanodots (CNDs), another kind of nanoparticles, have excellent advantageous properties such as high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization and good biocompatibility [29–31]. Thus, CNDs can be primely applied in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, medical diagnosis, photocatalysis and sensors [30–33]. However, most of these methods can only detect a single substance. Reports about the determination of dopamine and Al3+ by using CNDs based probes are rare.

Herein, a facile and green process was designed to synthesize CNDs. The CNDs can be used for multicolor bioimaging because of broad emission spectra with adjustable excitation wavelength. More significantly, the prepared CNDs exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity to dopamine and Al3+ with a detection limit of 0.0078μM and 0.0329μM, respectively. The present method was also successfully applied to the determination of dopamine in human urine and serum samples and Al3+ in food samples. Due to these unique properties, logic gates using CNDs as a new material were also constructed.

Citric acid anhydrous (99.8%), 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (97%), dopamine (98.0%) and ethanol (99.7%) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (99.7%), fetal bovine serum (FBS) (99.9%), trypsin (98.0%) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (98.0%) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water distilled by our own school was used throughout the experiments (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffered solution (10mM) was prepared by using a mixture Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 (purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) (Tianjin, China). The solutions of metal ions were prepared from NaNO3 (99.0%), KNO3 (99.0%), AgNO3 (99.8%), MgSO4 (99.0%), Mn(NO3)2 (99.5%), Co(NO3)2 (99.0%), Ni(NO3)2 (98.0%), CuCl2 (99.5%), Zn(NO3)2 (99.5%), Cd(NO3)2 (99.0%), Ba(NO3)2 (99.0%), PdCl2 (98.5%), Hg(NO3)2 (98.0%), Pb(NO3)2 (99.0%), Fe(NO3)3 (98.5%), Ce(NO3)3 (99.0%), Ca(NO3)2 (99.5%), La(NO3)3 (98.5%), Cr(NO3)3 (98.5%) and Al(NO3)3 (99.0%) (purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) (Tianjin, China).

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer from Tianjin Gangdong Scientific and Technological Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (GENESIS 60S from American EDAX) (Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on an H-7650 electronic microscope of HITACHI from Japan (Shanghai, China). UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Purkinje General TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer from Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Fluorescence emission spectroscopy was carried out on an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Tianjin gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). A PHS-3W pH meter (Shanghai Lida Instrument Factory) was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions (Shanghai, China). The fluorescence imaging was observed under an Olympus IX71 inverted fluorescence microscope from Japan with a 20× objective lens (Beijing, China).

According to the reported procedure, CNDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method [34]. Citric acid anhydrous (1.92g) and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (6.81g) were dissolved in deionized water (20mL). Then the solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was first neutralized with a Na2CO3 solution. Then solid was obtained by rotary evaporation and redissolved in ethanol. The insoluble precipitate in the solution was removed by centrifugation and got the supernatant liquid. The solution was further purified through loading into dialysis bags (Mw=3000) for dialysis against distilled water for 4days. Eventually, pure CNDs were obtained by rotary evaporation.

Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) was cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. For imaging, HUVEC cells were seeded on a cover-slip in 6-well plate 12h before use. The culture medium was replaced by 2.5mL fresh medium containing 10μgmL−1 CNDs and the cells were incubated for another 24h. After being washed with fresh phosphate buffered solution (pH=7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on the inverted fluorescence microscope.

The CNDs were dispersed in phosphate buffered solution (10mM at pH7.4) to form a concentration of 1mgmL−1. In a typical assay, 0.1mL CNDs and different amounts dopamine (1mM) were added into the phosphate buffered solution to form a 10mL clear solution. After mixing evenly, fluorescence measurements were carried out at excitation wavelength of 340nm with excitation and emission slit width of 5 and 10nm, respectively. The selectivity of dopamine was confirmed by adding other related analogues (0.1mL of a concentration of 2mM) instead of dopamine under the same conditions. All samples were prepared at room temperature.

For the typical assay, 0.1mL CNDs solution and 1mL dopamine solution (1mM) were added into 0.1mL of a concentration of 2mM metal ions solution respectively to form a 10mL clear solution with phosphate buffered solution (at pH7.4). Standard aqueous solutions of Hg(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, NaNO3, Mn(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, KNO3, La(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)3, Ca(NO3)2, AgNO3, Ba(NO3)2, Ce(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3, CuCl2, PdCl2 and Mg(NO3)2 were prepared in water with concentration of 2mM. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after 0.5h reaction. The fluorescence measurements were carried out with excitation and emission slit width of 5 and 10nm at excitation wavelength of 340nm. All samples were prepared at room temperature.

Citric acid, an important organic acid, is an important raw material for synthesizing CNDs through bottom-up carbonization routes [35,36]. The quantum yield can be enhanced through surface passivation. 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine is used as the passivation ligand to synthesize fluorescent CNDs. At the beginning, the carboxyl groups of citric acid polymerized with amine to form ester polymers. Then the polymers were further carbonized into carbon nanoparticles during the hydrothermal process. The result coffee brown powders indicate the successful carbonization of the raw materials. Then TEM was applied to reveal the morphologies of CNDs. It can be clearly seen that the nanoparticles are monodisperse with near spherical morphologies (Fig. 1a). Based on the statistical analysis of particles, the average size is about 3.9±0.5nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) picture in Fig. 1a reveals that the CNDs have a lattice spacing of approximately 0.32nm, which is consistent with the (002) planes of graphitic carbon [37]. FTIR spectrum was then taken to investigate the functional groups of CNDs (Fig. 1b). The peaks at 3430, 3228, 1656 and 1114cm−1 are corresponding with the vibrations of OH, NH2, CO and CO, respectively. These hydrophilic groups (NH2 and COOH) explain the good water dispersibility of CNDs. Specifically, the peaks of NHCO (around 1400cm−1) suggest that the acylation reaction have been occurred during the preparation of CNDs.

Then XPS analysis was used to investigate the surface elements construction of CNDs (Fig. 1c). The three main peaks around 285, 400 and 532eV are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high resolution C1s is divided into three peaks of CC (285eV), CN (286eV) and CO (288eV), indicating the existence of carbon atoms connected to N and O heteroatoms (Fig. 1d). The N1s spectrum shows two peaks around 400eV, which are assigned to CNC and NH bands, respectively (Fig. 1e). The spectrum of O1s was split into three peaks, corresponding to the binding energy of CO, COH and COC groups (Fig. 1f) [38,39]. These results are consistent with the corresponding FTIR spectra.

The optical properties of CNDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). The UV–vis absorption peak at 210nm represents the typical π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [40]. In order to understand the exact optical properties of CNDs, fluorescence spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission spectra are generally broad and dependent on excitation wavelength, which can be explained by an “edge red-shift effect” [41]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence peaks shifts to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength is changing from 310 to 380nm. The maximum intensity is loaded at the wavelength of 423nm by the excitation wavelength of 340nm. This excitation-dependent emission behavior is consistent with previous fluorescence analyses [42]. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 9.9% with the quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1M H2SO4, λex=360nm) as a reference material.

Due to the broad fluorescence emission spectra, CNDs can be used for multicolor bioimaging. Then MTT assay on HUVEC cells with different concentrations of CND were carried out to explore their cytotoxicity. According to the assay, CNDs present low toxicity without obvious cell viability reduction (Fig. S1). Then HUVEC cells were used to explore the potential of the CNDs as a bioimaging agent. Apparently shown in Fig. 4, CNDs entered into the HUVEC cells and displayed bright and multicolor fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths, while the shape and viability of cells show no substantial change.

To further confirm the fluorescence stability of CNDs, the effects of temperature, pH, stabilization time and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CNDs were investigated in phosphate buffered solution. As shown in Fig. S2a, the temperature has no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of CNDs. For the pH impact, CNDs showed the strongest fluorescence emission intensity when pH value was 7.0, which is similar to the physiological pH environment (Fig. S2b). These results indicate that CNDs can be regarded as good candidates for potential biological applications. In addition, the CNDs retain excellent fluorescence intensity even after 30days exposing to the air (Fig. S2c). Besides, the concentration of KCl does not have any high effects on CNDs, which is helpful for CNDs to be used in practical applications (Fig. S2d). All these results showed that the prepared CNDs have remarkable stability, which are probable for real fluorescent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of CNDs for dopamine
Mechanism for detecting dopamine by CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of CNDs quenched by dopamine demonstrates that electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding have taken place. The hydrogen group of dopamine can be oxidized to benzoquinone by molecular ambient O2 in basic pH [43,44]. When the CNDs were irradiated by photons with energy greater than the band gap, the electron of conduction bond was transferred to the quinone acceptor, inducing fluorescence quenching of CNDs (Fig. 4 ). The effective electron transfer process accelerated the non-radiative electron/hole recombination of excitons. By plotting the relationship between the concentration of dopamine and fluorescence intensity of CNDs, a novel fluorescent probe was developed to the determination of dopamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of CNDs-dopamine for Al3+ and its possible mechanism

ce:section.ce:para: In the control experiment, the fluorescence of CNDs without dopamine has no obvious enhancement in the presence of Al3+ (Fig. 5c). This result clearly suggests that the fluorescence recovery is actually attributed to the stronger binding between dopamine and Al3+. The selectivity of CNDs-dopamine towards Al3+ was explored by testing their fluorescence response towards other common metal ions. Remarkably, only Al3+ results in a significant fluorescent signal enhancement (Fig. 5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dopamine in biological fluids
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical applications of CNDs for sensing dopamine and Al3+, their fluorescence performance in biological fluids and food samples were evaluated. Human urine and serum samples were used to reveal the practical application of CNDs in analysis of dopamine. As summarized in Table S1, the values of dopamine detected by CNDs are in great agreement with the chose spiked values. The recoveries implied that the coexistence of inorganic or organic components did not significantly affect the detection of dopamine in real samples. This result confirms the feasibility of CNDs for dopamine detection in biological fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and some kinds of food were used as real samples for detecting Al3+. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of the tap water and food samples spiked with known amounts standard Al3+ are satisfied. Obviously, the composition of tap water and food sample sources do not significantly interfere with the detection of Al3+, indicating that this sensing platform can be applied to selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ in real water and food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gates operation of CNDs towards dopamine and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular logic gates were exploited to investigate the extensive application of CNDs (Fig. 6 ). Then we developed novel individual logic gates by taking good advantage of the corresponding sensing studies of dopamine and Al3+ by CNDs. Dopamine and Al3+ are used as inputs and the fluorescence signal as output. The absence and presence of these organic and ionic inputs with CNDs defines as 0 and 1, respectively. The output defined as 1 and 0 corresponds to strong and weak fluorescence response. The simplest logic gate was achieved by giving dopamine as input to CNDs. The quenched fluorescence emission of CNDs at 423nm in the presence of dopamine indicates that the output is 0 (low). The other logic gate was preceded by giving dopamine and Al3+ as inputs to CNDs. The enhanced fluorescence emission of CNDs-dopamine at 423nm in the presence of Al3+ indicates that the output is 1 (high). These observations clearly confirm the logic gates behavior according to truth table.

In conclusion, a straightforward and facile strategy was designed to synthesize CNDs through hydrothermal method. The CNDs can be used to multicolor cell imaging for their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Most importantly, such CNDs can also be served as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine and Al3+ not only in aqueous solution but also in real samples. The initial fluorescence of CNDs was quenched with dopamine due to the electron transfer process. With the addition of Al3+, the stronger binding between Al3+ and dopamine leaded to the fluorescence recovery of CNDs. Otherwise, the fluorescent probe can be utilized as various logic gates at molecular level. Combing their excellent optical properties, low cytotoxicity and ease of cell imaging, it is anticipated that CNDs have promising applications in the field of biological imaging, food security and other practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21374078, 51303132) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (no. 15JCYBJC18100, 15JCTPJC59200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2016.05.123.
